title: Get started with datey
text: datey provides three S3 classes: datey – point time, stored day-fraction precision. durationy – duration years. datey_interval – half-open [start, end) time interval. values stored exact integers (units 1/534 360 year), arithmetic precise associative.

title: Get started with datey
text: start_day(), mid_day() end_day() position datey start, middle end calendar day: end day point start next, end_day(y, m, d) always identical start_day(y, m, d + 1): arbitrary position within day, from_ymdf() accepts day fraction 0 1:
code: start_day(2024, 3, 7)   # start of 7 March 2024 #> [1] 2024-03-07.0 mid_day(2024, 3, 7)     # middle of 7 March 2024 #> [1] 2024-03-07.5 end_day(2024, 3, 7)     # end of 7 March 2024 #> [1] 2024-03-08.0 identical(end_day(2024, 3, 7), start_day(2024, 3, 8)) #> [1] TRUE from_ymdf(2024, 3, 7, 0.25)   # one quarter of the way through 7 March 2024 #> [1] 2024-03-07.25

title: Get started with datey
text: as_start_day(), as_mid_day() as_end_day() convert Date, POSIXct POSIXlt values, fixing day fraction: Date carries time component, datey(d) equivalent as_start_day(d).

title: Get started with datey
text: datey() also accepts fractional calendar year character string YYYY-MM-DD[.f] format:

title: Get started with datey
text: $ operator extracts components datey: several components needed , to_ymdf() efficient: .double() converts fractional calendar year; .integer() gives calendar year: is_start_day() is_mid_day() test position within day. Note end_day() produces datey start following day, tests is_start_day():
code: t <- mid_day(2024, 3, 7) t$year #> [1] 2024 t$month #> [1] 3 t$day #> [1] 7 t$day_fraction #> [1] 0.5 to_ymdf(t) #> $year #> [1] 2024 #>  #> $month #> [1] 3 #>  #> $day #> [1] 7 #>  #> $day_fraction #> [1] 0.5 as.double(t) #> [1] 2024.182 as.integer(t) #> [1] 2024 is_start_day(start_day(2024, 3, 7))      # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_mid_day(mid_day(2024, 3, 7))          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_start_day(end_day(2024, 3, 7))        # TRUE: end = start of next day #> [1] TRUE is_mid_day(from_ymdf(2024, 3, 7, 0.25)) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: Get started with datey
text: durationy() accepts number years: natural way obtain durationy subtract two datey values: .double() gives duration years; .integer() truncates toward zero:
code: durationy(1)      # one year #> [1] 1 yr durationy(0.5)    # half a year #> [1] 0.5 yr durationy(-2.5)   # two and a half years in the past #> [1] −2.5 yr dob <- as_start_day(as.Date("1965-09-12")) dod <- mid_day(2024, 3, 7) age <- dod - dob age #> [1] 58.485804 yr as.double(age) #> [1] 58.4858 as.integer(age)   # whole years only #> [1] 58

title: Get started with datey
text: table summarises typed arithmetic operations. arithmetic carried exact integer arithmetic underlying click counts, results precise associative.
code: start  <- start_day(2000, 1, 1) one_yr <- durationy(1) qtr_yr <- durationy(0.25)  start + one_yr          # one year later #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 start - qtr_yr          # three months earlier #> [1] 1999-10-01.75  one_yr - qtr_yr         # three quarters of a year #> [1] 0.75 yr one_yr + qtr_yr #> [1] 1.25 yr  datey(2024) < datey(2025)        # TRUE #> [1] TRUE durationy(1) > durationy(0.5)   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Get started with datey
text: datey_interval half-open [start, end) interval. Create one datey_interval() %% operator: $start, $end $duration properties extract interval’s components: durationy() accepts datey_interval directly:

title: Get started with datey
text: %includes% tests whether datey falls inside interval. interval includes start excludes end:
code: iv %includes% a                      # TRUE  -- start is included #> [1] TRUE iv %includes% b                      # FALSE -- end is excluded #> [1] FALSE iv %includes% mid_day(2024, 6, 15)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Get started with datey
text: is_proper() returns TRUE start ≤ end; is_collapsed() returns TRUE start ≥ end. point interval [, ) proper collapsed (contains time):
code: is_proper(iv)          # TRUE: start < end #> [1] TRUE is_collapsed(iv)       # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  pt <- a %to% a         # empty (point) interval is_proper(pt)          # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_collapsed(pt)       # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Get started with datey
text: & operator returns intersection two datey_intervals. direct way compute overlap two time periods:
code: period    <- start_day(2023, 7, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 6, 30) year_2024 <- start_day(2024, 1, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 12, 31)  overlap <- period & year_2024 overlap #> [1] [2024-01-01.0, 2024-07-01.0)  durationy(overlap)   # exposure in calendar year 2024, in years

title: Get started with datey
text: type dedicated NA constant: .na() anyNA() work expected: default (strict = TRUE), --range inputs stop execution. strict = FALSE become NA instead: NA values propagate arithmetic:
code: NA_datey_ #> [1] <NA> NA_durationy_ #> [1] <NA> NA_datey_interval_ #> [1] <NA> is.na(NA_datey_) #> [1] TRUE anyNA(c(datey(2000), NA_datey_, datey(2024))) #> [1] TRUE datey(999.9, strict = FALSE)     # below valid range: NA #> [1] <NA> durationy(2001, strict = FALSE)  # exceeds 2000-year limit: NA #> [1] <NA> start_day(2024, 1, 1) + NA_durationy_ #> [1] <NA>

title: The datey specification
text: datey system provides standard mapping dates onto annual grid. matters contexts primary unit years input data uses dates (.e. days)1. goal datey provide performant date duration-related arithmetic intervals input dates. following non goals handled outside datey system: Real-world date arithmetic2, including calculating dates output. Parsing formatting dates.

title: The datey specification
text: double means IEEE 754 binary64, .e. 64-bit binary floating-point. integer means 32 bit two’s complement signed integer. Dates written specification using YYYY‑MM‑DD notation, YYYY four-digit year, MM two-digit month year, 01 12, DD two-digit day month, 01 31. Banker’s rounding3 means round double nearest integer unless fractional part ±0.5, case round nearest even integer. algorithms , also entails conversion integer, safe , cases, algorithms guarantee value convert lies within range integer.

title: The datey specification
text: datey system assumes following: calendar years duration. Within calendar year, days duration. Time granular, smallest indivisible unit click, 1 / 534 360 year. variation arising , instance, time zones, daylight saving time leap seconds scope – allowance must done outside datey system.

title: The datey specification
text: datey system defines two types: datey represents date. durationy represents difference two dates. types represented using clicks stored integer.

title: The datey specification
text: Dates mapped dateys number clicks since start notional year 0000 proleptic4 Gregorian calendar. datey otherwise represent date calendar year 1000 start calendar year 2999 treated invalid5 mapping dates datey.

title: The datey specification
text: durationy represents duration using clicks. durationy otherwise represent duration magnitude 2000 years treated invalid mapping durations durationy.

what: Mapping years to a datey or durationy
title: The datey specification
text: datey can defined direct duration years start notional year 0000 durationy can defined direct duration years follows: duration years represented numerical type lower precision double must first converted double. result invalid following apply: duration years number (NaN). datey, duration years less 1000 greater 3000. durationy, absolute value duration years greater 2000. Otherwise resulting number clicks duration years multiplied 534 360, rounded using banker’s rounding finally converted integer . Worked examples:

what: Mapping a date to a datey
title: The datey specification
text: mapping date specified calendar year, month day datey point within day also needs specified. typical use, expected start, middle end day specified, mapping including day-fraction specified full generality. mapping function integer year, month day, double day_fraction follows: Valid inputs year: integer 1000 2999 inclusive month: integer 1 12 inclusive day: integer 1 number days month specified year month day_fraction: double 0 1 inclusive special cases () 0999-12-31 day_fraction = 1 (b) 3000-01-01 day_fraction = 0 also treated valid (equality precise equality, .e. rounding). special cases, year, month, day day_fraction invalid result invalid. Determine whether year leap year using proleptic Gregorian calendar. Define clicks_per_day 1460 year leap year 1464 otherwise. Define integer days_to_start_of_month number days start year start month, allowing whether year leap year. Define integer day_count days_to_start_of_month + day − 1. Define integer fraction_clicks day_fraction × clicks_per_day rounded integer using banker’s rounding. resulting number clicks defined integer calculation year × 534 360 + day_count × clicks_per_day + fraction_clicks convenience, datey implementation also provide mapping datey start, middle end date defined : start_day(year, month, day) := datey_from_YMDF(year, month, day, 0.0) mid_day(year, month, day) := datey_from_YMDF(year, month, day, 0.5) end_day(year, month, day) := datey_from_YMDF(year, month, day, 1.0) datey_from_YMDF algorithm function year, month, day day_fraction. Worked examples:

what: Mapping a datey to a date and day-fraction
title: The datey specification
text: datey mapped clicks year, month, day day_fraction follows: clicks < 1000 × 534 360 clicks ≥ 3000 × 534 360 datey invalid therefore result also invalid. year clicks div 534 360, div means integer division rounded nearest integer. Define clicks_remaining clicks − year × 534 360. Define clicks_per_day 1460 year leap year 1464 otherwise. Define day_in_year clicks_remaining div clicks_per_day, div means integer division rounded nearest integer. Use day_in_year determine month day. Define day_fraction_clicks clicks_remaining − day_in_year × clicks_per_day. day_fraction day_fraction_clicks / clicks_per_day using double division.

title: The datey specification
text: following arithmetic operations defined applying specified operators underlying argument clicks using 32-bit two’s complement arithmetic. unary plus (+) minus (negation −) operators defined durationy: + operator returns argument unchanged. − operator changes sign clicks. required expected operations checked integer overflow intermediate calculations basis overflows unlikely occur within domain operation, .e. times periods date inputs already checked validity, key rationale datey facilitate performant calculations.

what: Mixed datey / durationy and numeric operations
title: The datey specification
text: convenience, binary operations also defined one operands datey duration numeric. first step convert datey duration operand years follows: datey durationy invalid result number (NaN). Otherwise result clicks converted double divided 534 360. required numeric operand converted double. entails loss precision error. following standard operations legal

title: The datey specification
text: datey system provides simple, standardised text input output format. complicated scenarios, dates parsed formatted outside datey system.

title: The datey specification
text: valid datey represented text format YYYY‑MM‑DD.FFF YYYY four-digit year, MM two-digit month year (starting “01” January) DD two-digit day month (first day month “01”), .FFF optional day fraction (‘.’ present least 1 digit must also provided). text outputs: fraction contains 3 decimal places (sufficient distinguish possible day fractions datey precision). option whether zero day fraction stated explicitly (e.g. ‘2000-01-01.0’). text inputs: Blank fractions treated zero. Arbitrarily long inputs e.g. 100 UTF-8 bytes rejected. Subject overall limit, fractional part can contain arbitrary number digits.

title: The datey specification
text: Unless durationy serialised, recommended durations parsed formatted using conventional means applied duration measured years. valid durationy represented text format SY.FFFFFF UUU S plus minus sign, .e. one ‘+’ (U+002B), true minus ‘−’ (U+2212) ASCII hyphen-minus ‘-’ (U+002D). Y number whole years .FFFFFF optional fractional part year, including ‘.’ represent decimal point. UUU unit name one year. UUU non-blank preceded space. UUU longer 20 UTF-8 bytes contain control characters. text outputs: optional whether ‘+’ (U+002B) plus sign included positive durations. (Default omit ‘+’). optional whether use true minus sign ‘−’ (U+002B) ASCII ‘-’ (U+002D) hyphen-minus character. (Default use true minus sign.) fraction contains contains 6 decimal places sufficient distinguish possible duration fractions datey precision. unit name user-specifiable. (Default ‘yr’.) text inputs: plus minus signs included parsed (including zero durations). unit user-specifiable. non-blank unit text must present preceded space. (default ’ yr’.) Arbitrarily long inputs e.g. 100 UTF-8 bytes rejected. Subject overall limit, fractional part can contain arbitrary number digits.

what: The unit is years, but the data are dates
title: Why datey?
text: Mortality rates, valuation assumptions many actuarial quantities defined per year. data applied – dates birth, dates death, policy anniversaries, valuation dates – measured days. Converting two seems like trivial, isn’t. Consider calculations: “Add one year” 2024‑02‑29. 2025‑02‑29, answer – 2025‑02‑28 2025‑03‑01? defensible, different tools (different people) choose differently. “Add half year” twice starting 2000‑01‑01. land point time single “add one year” step? day-based arithmetic, answer – result depends split year , order additions. single ad hoc calculation kind ambiguity curiosity. actuarial model combines exposure periods, runs sensitivities, reconciled audited, ’s problem: logical calculation, expressed two different equivalent ways, can produce two different numbers.

what: The datey approach: a fixed annual grid
title: Why datey?
text: datey picks one standardised, precise mapping dates onto annual grid, guarantees arithmetic grid exact associative. Every datey durationy stored internally count clicks, one click 1 / 534 360 year, number chosen 1/365 1/366 year, useful fractions days years, represented exactly. approach, date duration calculations reduce plain old integer arithmetic precise associative. practical consequence two-steps-vs-one-step problem simply arise: (+ d1) + d2 == + (d1 + d2) datey durationys d1, d2 – always, exactly, regardless leap years order operations. ’s guarantee datey built around, [specification][spec] sets precisely.
code: start <- start_day(2000, 1, 1)  half_year <- durationy(0.5) two_steps <- (start + half_year) + half_year one_step  <- start + (half_year + half_year)  two_steps #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 one_step #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 identical(two_steps, one_step) #> [1] TRUE

title: Why datey?
text: Actuarial calculations often involve asking “much period rate X apply?” – e.g. combining policy’s time risk period particular assumption set valid. datey represents time intervals datey_intervals, written start %% end. half-open, .e. [start, end), intervals, means consecutive periods interlock precisely without gaps double-counting. datey provides interval algebra work time intervals directly:
code: time_at_risk <- start_day(2023, 4, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 4, 1) rate_period_2024 <- start_day(2024, 1, 1) %to% end_day(2025, 12, 31)  # the part of the time at risk to which the 2024 rate applies overlap <- time_at_risk & rate_period_2024 overlap #> [1] [2024-01-01.0, 2024-04-02.0)  # ... as a duration in years, ready to multiply by an annual rate durationy(overlap$end - overlap$start) #> [1] 0.251366 yr

title: Why datey?
text: datey can represent position within day (fraction year), datey provides start_day(), mid_day() end_day() three points within day come often: start_day() – day included start. Use e.g. start period risk. end_day() – day included including end. often end risk periods specified. mid_day() – average, event death occurs halfway day recorded . Choosing consistently (rather , say, always using midnight) improves clarity accuracy events included time period.
code: y <- 2026L m <- 1L d <- 1L one_day_period <- start_day(y, m, d) %to% end_day(2026, m, d) one_day_period #> [1] [2026-01-01.0, 2026-01-02.0)  mid <- mid_day(y, m, d) mid #> [1] 2026-01-01.5  interval_includes(one_day_period, mid) #> [1] TRUE

title: Why datey?
text: keep guarantees simple dependable, datey narrowly scoped. right tool : General date arithmetic output, e.g. “date 3 months now” calendar shown user. Parsing formatting dates beyond simple YYYY-MM-DD[.fff] representation. Time zones, daylight saving time, leap seconds, different calendars. Packages like clock lubridate already . trade-deliberate: refusing general date library, datey can make precise, associative annual grid representation rate-related calculations, one well-defined answer regardless calculation structured.

what: Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
title: Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
text: datey package provides standardised mapping dates onto annual grid together precise date duration-related arithmetic. work primarily mortality rates time intervals defined year input data uses dates, precision important, worth considering using datey. Classic examples mortality experience analysis valuation life assurance annuities. Mortality rates defined per annum experience valuation data usually defined using dates (.e. days). benefits using datey : consistent framework mapping dates annual units. Even mighty CMI come cropper inconsistencies point. Fixed precision arithmetic. approach excludes common bugs arising imprecision floating point arithmetic[^FloatingPoint]. sufficiently common problem CRAN FAQ topic. , mortality-specific context, Terry Therneau wrote note problems floating point can cause users R surv package.1 Handling whether date means start, end day. may seem trivial systematic errors can accumulate end material. detail motivation datey, see datey?.

title: Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
text: can install development version datey GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("logmu-org/r-datey")

title: Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
text: hands-guide provided Get started.
code: library(datey)  # Create datey from a base R date ... start <- as_start_day(as.Date("2018-09-12")) # Include *all* start day  # ... or direct from year/month/day exit <- mid_day(2024, 3, 7) # Death is mid-day on average # ... or simply as a fractional calendar year t <- datey(2021.234) # A date in the middle of the exposure period  c(start, t, exit) # 2018-09-12.0 2021-03-27.4098 2024-03-07.5 #> [1] 2018-09-12.0    2021-03-27.4098 2024-03-07.5  interval <- start %to% exit # Exposure period interval # [2018-09-12.0, 2024-03-07.5) #> [1] [2018-09-12.0, 2024-03-07.5)  interval %includes% t # Test if a point in time is in an interval #> [1] TRUE  exit - start      # Duration of exposure period #> [1] 5.485804 yr interval$duration # Same thing #> [1] 5.485804 yr  interval$duration * 0.01 # Implicit conversion to years when mixed with numerics #> [1] 0.05485804

what: Create a datey aligned to the start, middle or end of the day specified by a fractional calendar year or another date type. — as_xxx_day
title: Create a datey aligned to the start, middle or end of the day specified by a fractional calendar year or another date type. — as_xxx_day
text: Accepted types : Numeric, interpreted calendar year, fractional part representing fraction year. instance, datey(2000.5) means halfway year 2000. base R date types (Date POSIXct POSIXlt). character, ISO 8601 extended format, .e. YYYY-MM-DD. datey, interpreted start, middle end day override. Beware as_end_day() add day datey already day boundary, even originally defined end day.

title: Create a datey aligned to the start, middle or end of the day specified by a fractional calendar year or another date type. — as_xxx_day

what: Convert a datey to calendar years (including fractional part) — as_years_datey
title: Convert a datey to calendar years (including fractional part) — as_years_datey
text: Converts datey calendar years, including fractional part represents proportion calendar year elapsed. example, start 2000-01-01 (, equivalently, end 1999-12-31), results 2000, middle calendar year 2000 results 2000.5. Note following: .numeric() .double(). .integer() gives calendar year, e.g. .integer(datey(2000.9)) 2000. .integer(x) .integer(.double(x)).

Examples actuarial mortality experience analysis valuation life assurance annuities, mortality rates defined per year experience valuation data use dates.

text: Maintainer: Tim Gordon tim.gordon@btinternet.com (ORCID) Authors: Tim Gordon tim.gordon@btinternet.com (ORCID)

what: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
title: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
text: package provides methods create datey following: double integer interpreted specified calendar year, fractional part representing fraction year. instance, datey(2000.5) means halfway year 2000. (means integer argument always indicates start calendar year, e.g. datey(2000L) start year 2000.) Date POSIXct POSIXlt interpreted fractional years. day_fraction argument provided day fraction determined hours, minutes, seconds. instance, datey(.POSIXct("2000-03-21 12:00")) means middle 2000-03-21. Note standard use, Date fractional part therefore means start day. instance, datey(.Date("2000-03-21 12:00")) means start 2000-03-21. character – day_fraction provided text format must ISO 8601 extended format, .e. YYYY-MM-DD. day_fraction provided text format must YYYY-MM-DD.FFF, .FFF day fraction must present even fraction 0, e.g. "2000-01-01.0" start 1 January 2000. datey interpreted optional day_fraction override. Note day_fraction 1 add day day boundary, even originally defined end day. lengths vector arguments must multiples . S3 generic.

title: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
code: datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # Default S3 method datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'datey' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'integer' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'double' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'Date' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'POSIXct' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'POSIXlt' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, ...)

title: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
text: x argument convert datey. day_fraction day_fraction override. Defaults NULL. day_fraction provided x used derive calendar year, month, day day fraction. day_fraction provided x used solely derive calendar year, month day, day_fraction provides position day. day_fraction must lie inclusive interval [0,1], 0 meaning start day, 0.5 meaning middle day, 1 meaning end day (identical start next day). strict calendar years less 1000 greater 3000 day fractions interval [0,1] handled. strict TRUE – default – execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. NA arguments result NA (stop execution) regardless strict. ... arguments (used package).

title: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
text: vector datey.

title: Create a datey from a calendar year (including its fractional part) or another date type — datey
code: datey(2000) #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 datey(2000.5) # Middle of a leap year #> [1] 2000-07-02.0 datey(2001.5) # Middle of a non-leap year #> [1] 2001-07-02.5 datey(as.Date("2020-01-02")) #> [1] 2020-01-02.0 datey(as.POSIXct("2020-01-02 12:00:00")) #> [1] 2020-01-02.5 datey(as.POSIXlt("2020-01-02 12:00:00")) #> [1] 2020-01-02.5  # Use `strict` to control error behaviour for invalid `datey`s: try(datey(999.9)) #> Error : The year is invalid. try(datey(3000.1)) #> Error : The year is invalid. datey(999.9, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA> datey(3000.1, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>

title: Create a datey_interval — datey_interval
text: Create datey_interval representing [start, end). closed-open ('clopen') intervals start <= t < end, .e. interval includes start excludes end. two syntaxes: operator: start %% end function: datey_interval(start, end) equivalent strict always operator version.

title: Create a datey_interval — datey_interval
code: datey_interval(start, end, strict = TRUE)  start %to% end

title: Create a datey_interval — datey_interval
text: start, end start (inclusive) end interval (exclusive). can type convertible datey. numbers elements lengths must multiples . strict NAs handled. strict TRUE – default – execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned start /end NA.

title: Create a datey_interval — datey_interval
text: vector datey_interval.

title: Create a datey_interval — datey_interval

what: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
title: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
text: Extract year, month, day day_fraction datey using syntax $year, $month, $day $day_fraction respectively. $year, $month $day integer, $day_fraction double.

title: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
code: # S3 method for class 'datey' x$name

title: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
text: x datey. name Must year, month, day day_fraction.

title: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
text: vector integer year, month day; vector double day_fraction.

title: Extract year, month, day or day_fraction from a datey — datey_properties
code: t <- mid_day(2001, 2, 3)   t$year #> [1] 2001   t$month #> [1] 2   t$day #> [1] 3   t$day_fraction #> [1] 0.5

what: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
title: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
text: package provides methods create durationy following: integer – value interpreted specified number years. double – value interpreted specified number years, rounded fixed precision durationy. means durationy(0.5) precise durationy(0.01) . datey_interval – duration interval. strict FALSE datey_interval$duration. durationy – value unchanged. S3 generic.

title: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
code: durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)  # Default S3 method durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'durationy' durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'integer' durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'double' durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' durationy(x, strict = TRUE, ...)

title: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
text: x argument convert durationy. strict years greater 2000 magnitude handled. strict TRUE – default – execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. NA arguments result NA (stop execution) regardless strict. ... arguments (used package).

title: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
text: vector durationy.

title: Create a durationy from an annual duration — durationy
code: durationy(1)    # One year #> [1] 1 yr durationy(0.5)  # Half a year #> [1] 0.5 yr durationy(-2.3) # Negative duration #> [1] −2.3 yr durationy(datey(2001) %to% datey(2002)) # From an interval  # Invalid durations: try(durationy(3000.1)) # default is strict = TRUE #> Error : Absolute value of years argument is greater than 2000. durationy(3000.1, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>

title: Integer constants — integer_constants
text: following integer constants may make code clearer.

title: Integer constants — integer_constants
code: valid_years_start  valid_years_end  valid_duration_years_max

title: Integer constants — integer_constants
code: datey(valid_years_start - 0.001, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>   datey(valid_years_start) #> [1] 1000-01-01.0   datey(valid_years_end) #> [1] 3000-01-01.0   datey(valid_years_end + 0.001, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>   durationy(-(valid_duration_years_max + 0.001), strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>

what: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
title: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
text: Test whether datey_interval, \([,b)\), includes datey \(t\), .e. \(\le t\) \(t \lt b\). %includes% operator syntactic sugar interval_includes(). NA interval treated empty NA date treated interval, methods guaranteed return TRUE FALSE.

title: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
code: interval_includes(interval, value)  interval %includes% value

title: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
text: interval datey_interval. value datey test inclusion.

title: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
text: vector logical corresponding whether interval includes value. Always TRUE FALSE – NAs result FALSE.

title: Whether a datey_interval includes a datey — interval_includes
code: t_2000 <- datey(2000)   t_2001 <- datey(2001)   t_2002 <- datey(2002)   t_2003 <- datey(2003)   t_2004 <- datey(2004)    interval <- t_2000 %to% t_2003   interval %includes% t_2000 #> [1] TRUE   interval %includes% t_2001 # Start of interval *is* included #> [1] TRUE   interval %includes% t_2002 #> [1] TRUE   interval %includes% t_2003 # End of interval *not* included #> [1] FALSE   interval %includes% t_2004 #> [1] FALSE   interval %includes% NA_datey_        # NAs are FALSE #> [1] FALSE   NA_datey_interval_ %includes% t_2004 # NAs are FALSE #> [1] FALSE   interval_includes(NA_datey_interval_, t_2002) # NAs are FALSE #> [1] FALSE    # Function syntax:   interval_includes(interval, t_2002) #> [1] TRUE

title: Properties of an interval. — interval_nature
text: Test whether intervals, \([,b)\), 'proper' 'collapsed': proper interval end start, .e. \(\le b\). collapsed interval start end, .e. \(\ge b\). NA interval treated collapsed improper. definitions imply following: collapsed interval empty improper. test empty interval, .e. \([,)\), test proper collapsed. methods guaranteed return TRUE FALSE, .e. NA (provided argument interval). Vector versions mapping element x TRUE FALSE: is_proper(x) tests whether elements x proper. is_collapsed(x) tests whether elements x collapsed. Scalar versions mapping x scalar TRUE FALSE: all_proper(x) tests whether elements x proper. all_collapsed(x) tests whether elements x collapsed. any_collapsed(x) tests whether least one elements x collapsed. S3 generic functions.

title: Properties of an interval. — interval_nature
code: is_proper(x)  # Default S3 method is_proper(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' is_proper(x)  all_proper(x)  # Default S3 method all_proper(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' all_proper(x)  is_collapsed(x)  # Default S3 method is_collapsed(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' is_collapsed(x)  all_collapsed(x)  # Default S3 method all_collapsed(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' all_collapsed(x)  any_collapsed(x)  # Default S3 method any_collapsed(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' any_collapsed(x)

title: Properties of an interval. — interval_nature
text: x interval test.

title: Properties of an interval. — interval_nature
text: is_XXX functions return logical vector corresponding property. all_XXX any_XXX functions return logical scalar.

title: Properties of an interval. — interval_nature
code: a <- datey(1999)   b <- datey(2000)   is_collapsed(a %to% b) #> [1] FALSE   is_collapsed(a %to% a) #> [1] TRUE   is_collapsed(b %to% a) #> [1] TRUE   is_collapsed(NA_datey_interval_) #> [1] TRUE   is_proper(a %to% b) #> [1] TRUE   is_proper(a %to% a) #> [1] TRUE   is_proper(b %to% a) #> [1] FALSE   is_proper(NA_datey_interval_) #> [1] FALSE

what: Get the start, end or duration of a datey_interval — interval_properties

title: Get the start, end or duration of a datey_interval — interval_properties
code: # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' x$name

title: Get the start, end or duration of a datey_interval — interval_properties
text: x datey_interval. name Must start, end duration.

title: Get the start, end or duration of a datey_interval — interval_properties
text: start end return vector datey; duration returns vector durationy.

title: Get the start, end or duration of a datey_interval — interval_properties
code: t_1 <- start_day(2001, 1, 1)   t_2 <- start_day(2002, 2, 2)   interval <- datey_interval(t_1, t_2)   interval #> [1] [2001-01-01.0, 2002-02-02.0)   interval$start #> [1] 2001-01-01.0   interval$end #> [1] 2002-02-02.0   interval$duration #> [1] 1.087671 yr

what: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
title: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
text: Valid datey system ranges: Valid dates start 1000 start 3000. Valid durations 2000 years less magnitude. Values outside ranges treated NA. .na() tests whether datey durationy NA element. anyNA() tests whether element datey durationy NA. convenience, constants NA_datey_ NA_durationy_ datey durationy versions NA respectively, integer constants describing valid ranges also provided. performance reasons, intermediate calculations may check NAs.

title: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
code: # S3 method for class 'datey' is.na(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey' anyNA(x, recursive = FALSE)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' is.na(x)  # S3 method for class 'datey_interval' anyNA(x, recursive = FALSE)  # S3 method for class 'durationy' is.na(x)  # S3 method for class 'durationy' anyNA(x, recursive = FALSE)

title: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
text: x datey durationy test NA. recursive Currently required FALSE (default).

title: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
text: .na() returns vector logical length x. anyNA() always returns TRUE FALSE, never NA never anything single value.

title: Whether datey or durationy are NA — is_NA
code: t <- c(NA_datey_, datey(2000), datey(999.99, strict = FALSE))   is.na(t) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE   anyNA(t) #> [1] TRUE    d <- c(NA_durationy_, durationy(1.5))   is.na(d) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE   anyNA(d) #> [1] TRUE

what: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
title: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
text: Tests whether date year leap year. years outside [1000,3000), returns NA. S3 generic. package provides methods : numeric types double integer (interpreted years), date types datey, Date, POSIXct POSIXlt.

title: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
code: is_leap_year(x, ...)  # Default S3 method is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'integer' is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'double' is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'datey' is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'Date' is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'POSIXct' is_leap_year(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'POSIXlt' is_leap_year(x, ...)

title: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
text: x vector date type numeric year. ... arguments (used package).

title: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
text: NA x interpretable year date, outside [1000,3000), TRUE x leap year, otherwise FALSE.

title: Is x a leap year? — is_leap_year
code: any(is_leap_year(c(1900, 1901, 2001))) #FALSE #> [1] FALSE all(is_leap_year(c(1904.1, 2000.5, 2004.9))) #TRUE #> [1] TRUE

what: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
title: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
text: methods always return scalar logical TRUE FALSE: is_datey() tests whether object datey. is_durationy() tests whether object durationy. is_datey_interval() tests whether object datey_interval.

title: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
code: is_datey(x)  is_datey_interval(x)  is_durationy(x)

title: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
text: x object test.

title: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
text: logical scalar indicating whether x datey, durationy datey_interval appropriate. Always FALSE TRUE; never NULL NA.

title: Is x a datey, durationy or datey_interval? — is_type
code: t <- datey(2000:2001) t #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 2001-01-01.0 is_datey(t) #> [1] TRUE is_datey(NULL) #> [1] FALSE is_datey(NA) #> [1] FALSE  d <- durationy(0:2) d #> [1] 0 yr 1 yr 2 yr is_durationy(d) #> [1] TRUE is_durationy(NULL) #> [1] FALSE is_durationy(NA) #> [1] FALSE  interval <- datey(2000:2001) %to% datey(2001:2002) interval #> [1] [2000-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0) [2001-01-01.0, 2002-01-01.0) is_datey_interval(interval) #> [1] TRUE is_datey_interval(NULL) #> [1] FALSE is_datey_interval(NA) #> [1] FALSE

what: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
title: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
text: is_start_day() checks whether x start end day. is_mid_day() checks whether x middle day. properties necessarily preserved duration n years added subtracted.

title: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
code: is_start_day(x)  is_mid_day(x)

title: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
text: x datey test.

title: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
text: vector logical. Invalid datey elements return NA.

title: Is a datey the start (or end) or middle of a day? — is_xxx_day
code: # Start and end days: t <- start_day(2000, 1, 1) t #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 is_start_day(t) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_mid_day(t)   # FALSE #> [1] FALSE t <- end_day(2000, 1, 1) t #> [1] 2000-01-02.0 is_start_day(t) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_mid_day(t)   # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  # Mid day: t <- mid_day(2000, 1, 1) t #> [1] 2000-01-01.5 is_start_day(t) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_mid_day(t)   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # Neither a start nor mid day: t <- from_ymdf(2000, 1, 1, 0.25) t #> [1] 2000-01-01.25 is_start_day(t) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE is_mid_day(t)   # FALSE #> [1] FALSE  # Invalids return NA is_start_day(NA_datey_) # NA #> [1] NA is_mid_day(NA_datey_)   # NA #> [1] NA  # Properties are not preserved: t <- start_day(2000,7,1) # Leap year is_start_day(t) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_start_day(t + durationy(1)) # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

what: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
text: unary - operator can applied durationy change sign. following available binary operations datey durationy operands, meaning:

title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
code: # S3 method for class 'datey_type' Ops(e1, e2)

title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
text: e1 First parameter. e2 Second parameter (missing unary operator).

title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
text: See table. essence subtracting two dateys results durationy, comparing two Ts results logical, adding subtracting durationy T results T, mixing durationy datey numeric operands first converts durationy datey years results standard numeric evaluation, T either datey durationy .

title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
code: t_2000 <- datey(2000) t_2001 <- datey(2001) d_0.5 <- durationy(0.5)  t_2000 #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 t_2001 #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 d_0.5 #> [1] 0.5 yr  t_2001 - t_2000 # `datey` - `datey` is a `durationy` #> [1] 1 yr t_2000 + d_0.5  # `datey` + `durationy` is a `datey` #> [1] 2000-07-02.0 t_2001 - d_0.5  # `datey` - `durationy` is a `datey` #> [1] 2000-07-02.0 t_2000 + 0.5    # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 2000.5 d_0.5 + d_0.5   # `durationy` + `durationy` is a `durationy` #> [1] 1 yr d_0.5 + 0.5     # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 1 d_0.5 * 2       # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 1  interval <- t_2000 %to% t_2001 interval #> [1] [2000-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0) interval %includes% t_2000 # TRUE -- start *is* included in an interval #> [1] TRUE interval %includes% (t_2000 + d_0.5) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE interval %includes% t_2001 # FALSE -- end is *not* included in an interval #> [1] FALSE

code: # S3 method for class 'durationy' as.character(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'durationy' format(x, include_plus = FALSE, use_true_minus = TRUE, year_unit = "yr", ...)  # S3 method for class 'durationy' print(   x,   include_plus = FALSE,   use_true_minus = TRUE,   year_unit = "yr",   max = NULL,   ... )

title: Format or print a durationy — text_from_durationy
text: x durationy print format. ... arguments. include_plus Whether include plus ('+') sign positive durations. Defaults FALSE. use_true_minus Whether use true minus sign ('−', U+2212) sign opposed ASCII hyphen (-, U+002D). Defaults TRUE. year_unit year unit name print. blank value followed space unit. 20 characters (UTF-8 bytes) contain control characters. Defaults "yr". max Numeric NULL, specifying maximal number entries printed. NULL, getOption("max.print") used. Defaults NULL.

title: Format or print a durationy — text_from_durationy
text: .character format return vector character.

title: Format or print a durationy — text_from_durationy
code: pos <- durationy(1)   neg <- durationy(-2.3)   format(pos) # "1 yr" #> [1] "1 yr"   format(pos, include_plus = TRUE) # "1 yr" #> [1] "+1 yr"   format(pos, year_unit = "") # "1" #> [1] "1"   format(neg) # "−2.3 yr" #> [1] "−2.3 yr"   format(neg, use_true_minus = TRUE) # "−2.3 yr" #> [1] "−2.3 yr"   format(neg, year_unit = "a") # "-2.3 a" #> [1] "−2.3 a"

what: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
title: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
text: function parses text datey. text NA NA returned. day_fraction provided text must ISO 8601 extended format, .e. "YYYY-MM-DD". day_fraction provided text must formatted "YYYY-MM-DD.FFF", ".FFF" optional day fraction. means e.g. "2000-01-01" represents start 1 January 2000. strict TRUE (default) non-compliant text (blank NA) stop execution. blank_is_NA TRUE blanks treated NA (regardless strict). lengths vector arguments x day_fraction must multiples .

title: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
code: # S3 method for class 'character' datey(x, day_fraction = NULL, strict = TRUE, blank_is_NA = FALSE, ...)

title: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
text: x Vector text items parsed. day_fraction day_fraction override. Defaults NULL. day_fraction provided x used derive calendar year, month, day day fraction. day_fraction provided x used solely derive calendar year, month day, day_fraction provides position day. day_fraction must lie inclusive interval [0,1], 0 meaning start day, 0.5 meaning middle day, 1 meaning end day (identical start next day). strict non-compliant text (including calendar years less 1000 greater 3000) handled. strict TRUE execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. Defaults TRUE. blank_is_NA Whether "" treated NA. blank_is_NA FALSE execution stopped (regardless strict). blank_is_NA TRUE "" results NA. Defaults FALSE. ... arguments (used package).

title: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
text: vector datey.

title: Parse text as a datey — text_to_datey
code: datey("2000-01-01") #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 datey("2000-01-01", day_fraction = 0) #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 datey("2000-01-01.5") #> [1] 2000-01-01.5 datey("2000-01-01", day_fraction = 0.5) #> [1] 2000-01-01.5  # Day fraction cannot be present # both in the text and as an argument: try(datey("2000-01-01.0", day_fraction = 0)) #> Error : Invalid datey text. Should be "YYYY-MM-DD".  # Handling blanks: try(datey("")) #> Error : Blank datey text (and blank_is_NA is FALSE). datey("", blank_is_NA = TRUE) #> [1] <NA>  # Invalids: try(datey("abc")) #> Error : Invalid datey text. Should be "YYYY-MM-DD[.FFF]", where "[.FFF]" is optional fraction with at least 1 digit. try(datey("0999-01-01")) #> Error : `year` is outside [1000,2999] and date is not 3000-01-01.0. datey("abc", strict = FALSE) # NA #> [1] <NA> datey("0999-01-01", strict = FALSE) # NA #> [1] <NA>

what: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
title: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
text: function parses text durationy. text NA NA returned. Valid text form "DDD.DDD" "DDD.DDD UUU" "DDD.DDD" duration years "D" decimal digits (fractional part required whole years), "UUU" unit text (defaults "yr"). blank space duration years. blank_is_NA TRUE blanks treated NA. strict TRUE (default) non-compliant text stop execution.

title: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
code: # S3 method for class 'character' durationy(x, strict = TRUE, blank_is_NA = FALSE, year_unit = "yr", ...)

title: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
text: x Vector text items parsed. strict non-compliant text (including values greater 2000 magnitude) handled. strict TRUE execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. Defaults TRUE. blank_is_NA Whether blanks treated NA. Defaults FALSE. year_unit year unit name expect. blank value expected followed space unit text. 20 characters (UTF-8 bytes) contain control characters. Defaults "yr". ... arguments (used package).

title: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
text: vector durationy.

title: Parse text as a durationy — text_to_durationy
code: durationy("10 yr") #> [1] 10 yr durationy("+10 yr") #> [1] 10 yr durationy("-10 yr") #> [1] −10 yr durationy("10", year_unit = "") #> [1] 10 yr durationy("10 a", year_unit = "a") #> [1] 10 yr  # Handling blanks: try(durationy("")) #> Error : Blank durationy text (and blank_is_NA is FALSE). durationy("", blank_is_NA = TRUE) #> [1] <NA>  # Invalids: try(durationy("abc")) #> Error : Invalid durationy text. Missing year digit. try(durationy("2000.000001 yr")) #> Error : Invalid durationy text. Cannot be more than 2000 years. durationy("abc", strict = FALSE) # NA #> [1] <NA> durationy("2000.000001 yr", strict = FALSE) # NA #> [1] <NA> durationy("2000.000000 yr") # This is valid #> [1] 2000 yr

what: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day
title: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day
text: Create datey start, middle end day specified calendar year, month day. lengths vector arguments must multiples .

title: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day
code: start_day(year, month, day, strict = TRUE)  mid_day(year, month, day, strict = TRUE)  end_day(year, month, day, strict = TRUE)

title: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day
text: year Calendar year. Valid years 1000 3000 (although legal date 3000 start 3000-01-01). month Month number calendar year, 1 representing January. provided double must integers. day Day number month, 1 representing first day month. provided double must integers. strict handle calendar years less 1000 greater 3000 day fractions interval [0,1]. strict TRUE – default – execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. NA arguments result NA (stop execution) regardless strict.

title: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day
text: vector datey.

title: Create a datey for the start, middle or end of a day — xxx_day

what: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
title: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
text: lengths vector arguments must multiples . to_ymdf() returns list year, month, day day_fraction breakdown datey, year integer [1000,3000], month integer [1,12], day integer [1,N], N number days month specified year month, day_fraction double [0,1) representing fraction day, e.g. 0 means start 0.5 means middle day. Alternatively, want one two components, use list-like syntax $year, $month, $day $day_fraction. datey constructed using end_day day_fraction = 1 to_ymdf() return start next day day_fraction = 0. from_ymdf() creates datey calendar year, month, day fraction. practice, prefer one start_day(), mid_day() end_day() clarity.

title: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
code: to_ymdf(datey)  from_ymdf(year, month, day, day_fraction, strict = TRUE)

title: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
text: datey datey deconstructed. year Calendar year. Valid years 1000 3000 (although legal date 3000 start 3000-01-01). provided double must integers. month Month number calendar year, 1 representing January. provided double must integers. day Day number month, 1 representing first day month. provided double must integers. day_fraction fraction day, [0,1]. 0 means start day, 0.5 means middle day, 1 means end day (identical start next day). strict handle invalid arguments. strict TRUE – default – execution stopped. strict FALSE NA returned. NA arguments result NA (stop execution) regardless strict.

title: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
text: from_ymdf returns vector datey. to_ymdf returns list integer vector year, integer vector month, integer vector day, double vector day_fraction.

title: Create or decompose a datey using calendar year, month, day and day fraction — ymdf
datey 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. datey durationy represent dates durations fixed-precision annual grid, exact, associative arithmetic (+, -, comparisons, mean(), range(), etc.). start_day(), mid_day(), end_day() from_ymdf() map calendar year/month/day (day-fraction) datey; to_ymdf() as_*_day()/is_*_day() helpers provide reverse mapping checks. Conversion Date, POSIXct, POSIXlt, /years via .double()/.integer(). NA_datey_, NA_durationy_ NA_datey_interval_ provide type-correct NAs, full .na()/anyNA() support. Text parsing formatting datey, durationy datey_interval. pillar_shaft() methods readable printing datey/durationy/ datey_interval columns tibbles. is_leap_year() integer years dateys. datey_interval (constructed datey_interval() %%) represents half-open [start, end) date interval, %includes%, is_collapsed(), is_proper(), intersection via &, constants all_of_time NA_datey_interval_.
